content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions month

## 1. Primer (2004) is a genius, low-budget sci-fi that demands your full attention.

**Score:** 394
**Body:** This is one of the most meticulously crafted time-travel movies ever made. It was shot on a tiny budget but feels incredibly smart and complex.  
You'll need to watch it more than once to piece everything together, and that's the best part of the experience.

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I watched this once, thought I was following it, then in a moment had no idea what was happening
  - Being an edgy teen i was like “i fully understand this movie”. Then i opened the graphic someone made of all the timelines and i was oblivious to 95% of them lol

One of my first big humblings in life 
  - it's pretty much impossible to follow. i will preface by saying i think primer is the best time travel movie ever made, but it's biggest strength is also it's biggest flaw; it's just too complex. as it should be, multiverse time travel theory is crazy. it also means the movie has great rewatch value, but you pretty much need to either watch an explanation after or take notes while watching and really scrutinize every word. the script is very tight and fast so every line of dialogue has purpose, and they don't ever give you a moment to breathe and digest, which just makes it even harder to follow. when you do get it though, it is so rewarding.
  - I've seen it more than once, but same thing
    - I've seen it like, 6 times, watched youtube videos explaining it, studied infographics. . . and yeah, same thing
      - My favorite review of this film said "I don't want to be spoon-fed, but at least unlock the kitchen door for me!"
2. One of the greatest science fiction kicked ever made. Doubly impressive that is was made for only $7000.

Primer was the only movie which I've ever rewatched immediately after watching it for the first time. Then I watched it three more times. After all that I think I understand about 80% of it.
3. The dvd with the director commentary is key to understanding that movie. I love it 
4. The director was on his way to being a new Kubrick IMHO, but ended up stalking his girlfriend/starlet and has since disappeared. If I'm wrong does anyone have updates on what he's involved in?
  - Yeah it’s such a disappointment that he disappeared under dodgy circumstances. I love Upstream Color maybe just as much as Primer, and then I read his screenplay for ‘A Topiary’ I think it was called. It was incredible. That would have been of the charts if he could have gone on to make it. Although I think he described it as impossible to make anyway.

## 2. Looking for a movie in which main character will die, without happy end

**Score:** 339
**Body:** I'm tired of all the cliched movies where the main characters always end up alive and happy. I'd like a more tragic and realistic situation, where ultimately there's no happy ending and the main character dies (or at least that question remains open). The genre is not important. Something like: (spoilers !) >!Taxi Driver, Drive, Midsommar, Birdman, Atonement, Lilya-4Ever, La Haine.!<

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Pan’s Labyrinth
  - The sick part about that one is you know, from the very beginning, what happens. Then you forget. Then del Toro slaps you across the face and yells, "I already TOLD you. Remember?!"
2. Rogue One.
3. Grave of the Fireflies

Fun fact: the family sitting behind me when i first saw it thought it was a fun kids movie, and brought their 5 year old. They were all arguing in the way to the car.
4. No country for old man
5. Braveheart

Gladiator 

Thelma and Louise

Children of Men

Titanic
6. The Road. Some find the ending hopeful some do not but overall it will definitely give you the despair you are seeking
7. Bone Tomahawk. The Guest. Requiem for a Dream.
8. To Live And Die In L.A. (1985)
9. The Mist is *almost* what you're looking for.

## 3. Moon (2009) is a masterclass in minimalist sci-fi and a truly haunting character study.

**Score:** 262
**Body:** This is one of those films that proves you don't need a massive budget to tell a profound and gripping story. Sam Rockwell's performance is absolutely incredible—he carries the entire film on his shoulders.  
It's a quiet, intelligent, and deeply isolating movie that explores identity and sanity in a way that sticks with you long after it's over.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Directed by Duncan Jones, David Bowies son. I think it’s a fantastic movie!
  - Forgot about that! It's been ages since I've seen it. Gotta watch again.
2. Just watched this for the first time a few weeks ago. Sam Rockwell is phenomenal, and the movie is just perfect.
3. Fantastic score too. One of Clint Mansell's best.
4. Everything about the movie works. The concept is fantastic, blending a bunch of different types of plausible Sci-Fi. The script is tight and nearly flawless. Rockwell is pitch-perfect in both roles in every scene, and Spacey's delivery balances the flatness of an AI with just the right notes of emotion. The tension is perfectly balanced. The mystery is unraveled really well. The music is brilliant. The production design is amazing, especially for the budget. I was so glad to have seen it in theaters when it came out.
5. I feel like this movie has become a little bit forgotten since Interstellar came out, but it was quite popular before then. I'll have to rewatch it one of those days, I remember it being quite funny without being a comedy as well.
6. I saw this when it came out in theaters and was completely awed. Been a fan of Sam Rockwell ever since.
7. No need to use AI, I'd appreciate you telling us what you think in your own words
  - Why would someone use AI for three sentences?
8. Totally agree. I should do a re-watch soon.

## 4. Just watched the new "Frankenstein"

**Score:** 244
**Body:** Hey everyone,  
I just got out of the theater after watching the new "Frankenstein" movie, and I wanted to drop a quick recommendation for anyone on the fence.

Without spoiling anything, the film does a great job balancing the classic gothic vibe with more modern themes. It’s not just a retelling, it actually adds something fresh without disrespecting the original story. The atmosphere, the creature design, and the emotional weight all hit way harder than I expected.

If you’re into sci-fi horror, slow-burn tension, or thoughtful character-driven stories, this is absolutely worth seeing. It’s not your typical jumpscare fest; it’s more about mood, tragedy, and the consequences of playing god. Also, the performances were way better than I thought they'd be especially the actor playing the creature.

I’d say: go in with an open mind, don’t expect a straight-up action-horror flick, and you’ll probably enjoy it as much as I did.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I saw it this weekend and thought it was beautiful.  I think anyone looking for horror will be disappointed. It does have an old atmospheric horror feel to it but no real scares that I can think of.
  - Del Toro has always been atmospheric horror.
  - I went in expecting jump scares and left thinking about life and monstrosity instead.
  - That’s what I expected more gothic mood less actual horror
2. Don’t forget the generational trauma and how it’s passed down from father to son, not once but twice! Loved that.
3. Del Toro has a way of making movies that feel artistic and fantastical while you watch them, but after the movie you continue to think about it and it begins to feel like like horror as the ideas sink in
4. It’s not about the consequences of playing god it’s about rising above and not becoming the monster people claim you to be. Like always the movie is about the monster not Frankenstein
5. I watched it last night on Netflix and liked it too. I bet it was great to watch it in the theatre.
6. I thought it was incredibly beautiful and am already on my second watch.

I was shocked by Jacob Elordi. Up until now I'd pretty much dismissed him as Hollywood's latest jock prettyboy (although I did think he was good in Saltburn) but holy shit am I eating my words. He was outstanding as the creature, he made me cry several times.

I'm not sure how I feel about the departures from the novel, but it was still a gorgeous film.

Now please please please we need a GDT Phantom of the Opera (the original Leroux, not the Lloyd Webber.) Guillermo has said he wants to do it, make it happen.
7. Excellent movie, brilliant visuals and settings. Definitely worth watching. Loved Oscar's performance. One thing I disliked was waltz's character arc before his death. Felt like it happened for the sake of happening. Waltz was definitely under utilised in this movie.

## 5. I need to get off my phone and watch movies again. Recommend me something, if it gets 10 upvotes, I’ll watch it and reply with what I thought of it.

**Score:** 201
**Body:** I feel like I haven’t watched anything good lately, most of the time I’m half-watching some trash movie or TV shows while scrolling on my phone. I need some strong recommendations. I’m open to pretty much anything.

Drop your best pick and I’ll give it a try, then come back with my feedback.

**Edit:**

Holy s\*\*t, I wasn’t expecting so many of you guys to give me movie suggestions. I am a man of my word though, and I’ll be watching every single movie that gets 10 upvotes or more. I’ll be watching at leas one movie a day until the list is completed, and I’ll reply to your comment before I watch the movie and again afterward with my thoughts on it.

The order in which I’ll be watching the movies will be decided by the number of upvotes, so I’ll go from the most upvoted comment to the least. So stay tuned and be patient, the list is now in the mind-blowing range of 100+ movies, but your turn will come, I promise.

**Movies watched & reviews:**  
\- Children of men  
\- Office Space  
\- Little Miss Sunshine  
\- Best in Show  
\- Blade Runner  
\- My Cousin Vinny

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Tombstone 
2. Best In Show
3. Jackie Brown
4. Office Space 
5. Harold & Maude
6. Blade Runner.  Sci-fi isn’t usually my fav but this was good.

Goodfellas is always entertaining
7. My Cousin Vinny
8. Children of men
9. The Thing
10. The Silence of the Lambs

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic month

## 1. Actors that you loved as a kid, but grew up realizing there horrible people in real life.

**Score:** 11333
**Body:** Beetlejuice, Ferris Bueller,  Howard the duck.... this man was in some of my favorite 80s movies. Loved his characters, turns out he's a huge turd though in reality.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Stephen Collins (the dad from 7th Heaven). He even admitted to “sexual misconduct with minors” and the guy is still free with no repercussions, apparently.
  - Watching his “Save the Kids” song in Always Sunny and how much he kissed fucking EVERYONE in the show constantly makes me so uncomfortable now
    - It's an interesting juxtaposition of him being a piece of shit but his character being nice vs Danny Devito, whose character is a deranged degenerate but, from all accounts I have seen, is a lovely, kind person
2. Had no idea Jeffrey Jones is on the sex offender registry AND continued to get work after all of that until now. Disgusting.
3. OJ Simpson. The Naked Gun was legendary.
4. Not an actor but a director. Victor Salva, who made the Jeepers Creepers films, went to prison for sexual assault of a minor who starred in one of his movies, and still had a career after it happened. Nathan Forrest Winters, the child actor he groomed and abused after working with him on the film Clownhouse, has picketed his films and released a documentary about it. I never found out about this until I was an adult; Jeepers Creepers 1 & 2 were standard slumber party movies for me and my friends. The fact that there are convicted sex offenders who continue to get to not just work in Hollywood but work with children disgusts me.
5. Andy dick
  - Jon Lovitz did nothing wrong.
6. Danny Masterson. I legitimately wanted to be like Hyde when I was a kid and followed Danny's career because of that role.

Learning he was a rapist is an absolute shot to the gut.
  - I’m pretty sure the only main actor from that show that didn’t defend Danny was Topher Grace

## 2. An actor you had no expectations for but who turned out to be great.

**Score:** 9228

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I like that he doesn’t play the same character.
  - See, Dwayne? It IS possible!
2. I was hoping this was going to be a sequence where he was wearing glasses that kept getting tinier, but this is good, too.
3. He is the better actor among the WWE lot
  - I agree, but I am also really enjoying Cena in *Peacemaker*.
4. Heath Ledger as Joker. When I saw he was casted I was very disappointed. Never been so happy to be so wrong. RIP.
5. Robert Pattinson
  - The Lighthouse, The Batman, and TENET were all great performances I never would have expected from Edward Cullen.
    - Check out Good Time if you haven’t as well, another great performance and a wild ride of a movie.
6. I really liked him in James Bond

## 3. What's the most cruel thing you've seen in a movie?

**Score:** 8979
**Body:** One scene in particular I could think of is this one from 1408 with John Cusack where his character "reunites" with his daughter for a short amount of time. Won't say anything else to those who haven't seen it!

EDIT: Goddamn, I never thought my post would get so big lol.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. "She begged for her life and for the life of the baby inside her. Ohhhh, he didn't know."

- John Doe in Se7en
  - CALIFORNIA STAY AWAY FROM HERE. JOHN DOE HAS THE UPPER HAND
2. The Green Mile, Del’s execution.
3. Shoe, roger rabbit
  - The Dip!
  - STOP. Lol I block this one out for a few months at a time but it always randomly comes creeping back & I feel a sense of panic because I can't save him. I'm 42.
4. Baseball Boy from Dr. Sleep.

Horrible
5. In The Grey when the guy falls out of the tree and his daughter is standing over him with her hair draping over his face and then it cuts to the reality of wolves eating his face.
  - For me it’s the dude in the river who dies like an inch below the surface. Brutal
6. The end of Funny Games where they nonchalantly tossed that woman in the lake to drown. Even though the entire movie is about pointless cruelty, that last act struck me as particularly cruel and disrespectful.

## 4. Actors who suffer from Christoph Waltz Syndrome?

**Score:** 8655
**Body:** **Christoph Waltz Syndrome: great actors who were in some great films so producers assume they’ll naturally elevate a mediocre film.**

In the aftermath of *Inglorious Basterds* & *Django Unchained,* Waltz started to get cast in projects like *Tarzan.* While he’s very charismatic and engaging, an actor can only do so much with weak material. Simply casting someone like Christoph Waltz isn’t enough to bump your film up a notch. I think the most glaring example is *Spectre,* where Waltz is utterly wasted on a flat, shallow character who does little to nothing over the course of his screentime. 

These aren’t actors who simply take bad roles, they’re actors who are victims of their own success, somehow expected to be their own writer and director. Actors who were obviously shoehorned in thanks to a note from producers who don’t understand how filmmaking or acting works. 

As a side note, I’m not saying Waltz’s career has flatlined. He’s recently appeared in *Only Murders in the Building* in a role that perfectly suits him. It’s just a shame that he’s a supporting player in a streaming show - most of us can remember when he was meant to be the next Anthony Hopkins. 

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Christopher Lee said "You can be in a terrible film, but you can't be terrible in it" 

The man was never out of work
  - I mean have you seen Howling 2: Your Sister is a Werewolf?  Every time he appears on screen he absolutely outshines everyone.  

Also Tim Curry, Christopher Walken, and Patrick Stewart belong in this discussion.  Tim would be working like crazy if he didn't have that stroke.
2. I can’t believe they fumbled the Waltz Blofeld so badly.  It was solely in the writing.  Making him related to Bond was so immeasurably stupid.
  - Absolutely beyond dumb. But honestly, after what happened with Kahn and Reboot Trek, I wasn't really surprised.
3. Willem Dafoe, without a doubt. The man is an incredible actor and virtuoso but I've seen him in some bad films where he is the best actor in it. There are some films even his great acting cannot save. I think Gary Oldman, Max Von Sydow, Paul Giamatti and Steve Buscemi also qualify for this.
  - Eggers is giving Dafoe the weird and interesting series of roles his talent deserves.
4. The idea that all actors should be trying to get only the best roles and reject all others, to only seek acclaim, is to forget that they are artists and people with personal interests. A lot of the best artists don't care if they please everyone, because that is impossible. And part of being human is making mistakes, taking a part with a director they don't get along with, choosing a film that sounds good on paper but is executed poorly or ruined in the editing room.  

This sub often wants to eschew popcorn flicks, Marvel movies, because they are too corporate, too focused on commercial success. Then turns right around and criticizes actors who misstep and work on a film that is less than perfect, or fumble a single role, because it hurts their commercial success. Completely hypocritical. This is a perfect example of why actors shouldn't give a damn about what people think.
5. Giancarlo Esposito

Aidan Gillen

Bryan Cranston
6. Waltz was great in Big Eyes
7. F. Murray Abraham, by his own admission. Watched an interview with him once where he said after winning the Oscar, he’d received a ton of offers for ok films for good money filming in exotic locales. 

Basically used the opportunity to get paid while traveling around the world after being a journeyman actor forever, assured by his agents no one would see them. But, apparently, a lot of these movies were picked up by cable TV and it hurt his film career, so he went back to theater and he’s had a nice resurgence again on TV in recent years.

## 5. Oscar wins that felt undeserved

**Score:** 7488
**Body:** This is is a post to vent my disbelief and frustration at some of the most baffling Oscar wins. Let me get this started; Eddie Redmayne for The Theory of Everything (2014), Rami Malek for Bohemian Rhapsody (2019)

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. What about the fact that Bohemian Rhapsody also won for best editing
2. Gweneth Paltrow
3. Entire Emilia Perez
  - this is funny but i completely forgot this movie ever existed until seeing your comment
4. Robert Shaw not even being nominated for Quint in Jaws just shows how shite these awards are
  - Also, Wes Studi as Magua in Last of the Mohicans. You don't even think he's acting, you just genuinely believe that is Magua. No Oscar nomination.
    - Wes Studi is a name i drop and im like , "trust me, you know who he is. " Heat, Deep Rising, fucking Mystery Men!. Dudes a bad ass
5. Oscar voters don't watch all of the movies.
6. Shakespeare in Love. 
It was charming and entertaining, but it was no Saving Private Ryan. Not by a long shot.  

Fucking Weinstein. 
  - Will never understand Gwyneth getting the nod over Cate.

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm month

## 1. Frankenstein (2025): A film that robs it’s subject matter of it’s poetic nature

**Score:** 874
**Body:** Up until now, I’ve had great hopes for Frankenstein (2025). I’m quite a Del Toro fan, with *Pan’s Labyrinth* and the *Hellboy* films being particular favourites. Del Toro has always had an affinity for finding the humanity in monsters we would typically consider grotesque, but here, Del Toro allowed his love for the monster to get the better of him, to the point of removing any nuance or contrast within this character. 

As a fan of the original Mary Shelley novel, the contrast between this monstrous being and an incredibly tragic backstory has always been fascinating. The fact that such a grotesque, monstrous creature is an abandoned, vulnerable child lent the novel a horrifically poetic quality. But while Del Toro’s adaptation has received praise for ‘humanising’ The Creature, my experience of watching the film left me with the conclusion that Del Toro went too far in the opposite direction, to the point of robbing the character of the contrasts which have made him such a fascinating character across decades.

The Creature, as played by Jacob Elordi, is barely a monster. He rarely commits any violent actors throughout the film, and in 95% of instances, any violent acts are committed in complete self-defence. Instead of making you question who the real monster is, the film draws its conclusion for you, with characters literally calling Doctor Frankenstein the real monster. But the biggest misstep for me is the design of the Creature. Jacob Elordi is hard to fault in his performance, but instead of allowing his eyes alone convey the vulnerability of the character, The Creature essentially looks like Jacob Elordi in white makeup (with a green hue). The effect this leaves us with, is that whenever Elordi is on the screen, we barely feel like we’re watching a monster, or an experiment gone wrong.

I admire Del Toro’s ambitions here, but in the process of humanising Frankenstein more than any previous adaptation, he has lost something crucial. It leaves you with a lingering question: At what point has the monster simply become too human? And in robbing Frankenstein’s Monster of the crucial poetic tension that exists between his humanity and his grotesque nature, how much of a disservice has he done to the character?

EDIT: [Here](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OZGbDlfuK2U) is my full review where I get more in-depth into the topic, if anyone is interested.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I enjoyed reading that and I'm looking forward to seeing the film. The reviews I've read seem to echo what you're saying.

I wonder if GDT just loves his monsters a bit too much? The monster in Frankenstein (the novel) is a sympathetic character, but he is very much still a monster. He does kill innocent people. GDT's approach to humanising his monsters is in danger of becoming a bit of a schtick. Why can't they be sympathetic AND monstrous, in the way that humans can be? That to me is much more interesting than just a misunderstood ugly guy who only kills in self defense.
  - While I take OP's point (though I really liked the film overall), saying the monster only kills in self-defense is a bit of a stretch. He does kill a number of crew on the ship. They shoot at him first, but he then *advances* on the ship, killing crew as he tries to get to Viktor.
  - Well said! Feels like in his attempt to humanise the Creature, he only made him more one-dimensional. Hope to hear your thoughts once you watch the film.
    - You nailed the characterization of the source material. 
To capture the tragedy of the monster, he has to be utterly hideous. The de lacey family arc is so important in teaching that harsh lesson to the monster, no matter what he says or does, he will never be given a chance. The father/god vs son/creation paradigm is what takes my breathe away every single time I read it. 

Vitor utterly failed his son over and over and over again, he broke down the sparks of humanity in his creation, and sought to blame him for all the death/destruction victor caused. 

If that’s no intact, I can’t see myself loving the new film. 
I still appreciate every adaptation attempt, but after two centuries, you’d think someone would have worked it out haha 

At least we have Junji ito
2. I more or less agree here. I feel similar to Coppola’s Dracula. Where it’s probably my favorite adaptation of the book, and it’s very beautiful, but I loathe the changes/interpretations made to it. I was really hoping for a loyal adaptation of the novel but this is not that. 

To me, I think the whole focus on whether or not it’s a “monster” is missing the point though. I just reread the book and the creature shows a knack for deep introspection and petty violence. Ironically,  sounds pretty human to me. I agree with you that they shouldn’t have strayed away from the creature’s darkness, because through that we actually explore the human condition more. Where we are both capable of great good and great evil. The film seems like it’s trying to take a black and white stance and beats you with “he’s a victim! the doctor is the real monster!” That’s not that interesting to me. 

Overall, very well put. I do recommends everyone sees it though. Definitely not a bad movie. 
3. It did also lend a certain absurdity to every instance the monster was attacked without provocation.

"What... is that?"

"I think... a reasonably tall man in a cloak? If I squint, I think he has some facial scarring?"

"Unload on it!"
  - This is unfortunately true.
4. I'm torn on this film. I had very high hopes going in since I consider Del Toro's Pinocchio a masterpiece, or very close to it. I assumed if he could do that with Pinocchio, he would have no problems adapting Frankenstein into an amazing modern visual story. During the first ~25 minutes of the film I was loving every moment of it. As the story unfolded, I became less enthralled. I agree with your point that the creature is barely a monster, I remember sitting there thinking "why did they make Frankenstein hot?" 

Overall I still very much enjoyed the film and would probably give it a solid 4/5, but IMO it was not the 5/5 I was hoping for. That being said, it's definitely worth a watch despite it's tiny flaws. The cinematography was amazing and the pacing never felt too slow or too fast.
5. Well put! I'll add that robbing the creature of its monstrous nature weakens Victor's inner torment and makes him a less interesting character by extension. Instead of facing the "What monstrosity have I created?" problem, Victor is left with a less compelling and more convoluted, >!"What monstrous deeds have I done in order to create this really cool thing that fails to meet my expectations...which, actually, in the end, turns out to perfectly meet my expectations except that it's way more likeable than I am, dammit, and maybe even hotter?"!<

On a psychological level, I heard someone muse that Del Toro's leading man creatures are self-inserts, which makes sense why he'd remove Frankenstein's Creature's more horrifying qualities.
6. Interesting writeup. To me, the best depiction of the Monster has been Penny Dreadful. We know he's a monster capable of horrible things we see him do, but there's a sadness and poetry to him that makes him sympathetic. And Rory Kinnear is fantastic. 

## 2. Why did Darren Aronofsky's Caught Stealing come and go so quietly?

**Score:** 483
**Body:** I’m genuinely baffled by how Caught Stealing came and went without much noise. You’d think a Darren Aronofsky film starring Austin Butler and Zoë Kravitz would do better, especially a crime thriller this stylish and pulpy. But alas, that's the state of modern cinema. But audiences were also pretty lukewarm or unreceptive. Instead, it felt like a blip, even though it might be his most accessible and purely fun film in years. Obviously, blame it on Sony for refusing to give this one a festival bow and sticking it in the dog days of August. A director friend of mine who adores Aronofsky didn't even know he had a new film out this year. 

But the more I sat with it, the more it struck me that Caught Stealing isn’t just a mild detour for Aronofsky. It’s a film where his long-standing obsessions (sin, guilt, moral reckoning, divine punishment) collide with a real sense of play and fun. It’s Hitchcockian in precision and got a Coen-esque rhythm, which is a departure for him but still under his signature umbrella. 

What really stands out is how controlled it is beneath the chaos. Every twist and tonal shift feels deliberate, not indulgent. The humor never undercuts the tension. Aronofsky has often been accused of self-seriousness, but here, his control of tone is remarkable. 

What I also love is how tactile the film feels. 1998 New York isn’t treated like a nostalgia trap but a real, breathing city. The bars, the grime, the music, the fashion all pulse with life. Aronofsky doesn’t romanticize the era; he immerses you in it. 

It’s also one of the few recent films that really feels like it has movie stars again. Austin Butler channels that old-school charisma but grounds it with vulnerability. He’s magnetic without ever winking at the audience. Kravitz, meanwhile, gives my favorite performance of hers (although, hot take, I'm not a massive fan of hers). Together, they give the film an energy that reminds you why we go to the movies in the first place.

And yet, for all that, hardly anyone’s talking about it.

I would blame most of it on the marketing and the late-August release, but even those who caught (pun) it didn't really sing its praises that I believe are earned. Perhaps some viewers didn’t know what to make of a filmmaker known for Requiem for a Dream and The Whale delivering a crime comedy with a beating moral heart. And for cinephiles, one may summise that the heaviness of his recent films weighed on their decision not to seek this one out. 

I’d love to hear what others thought. Did Caught Stealing deserve more conversation? Do you see it as a minor work, or is it secretly one of his most confident films?

I wrote a full review, digging deeper into these questions and Aronofsky’s worldview in the film, if you want to check out my expanded thoughts, but put your thoughts down below!!  
[https://open.substack.com/pub/abhinavyerramreddy/p/caught-stealing-surprise-after-surprise?r=38m95e&utm\_campaign=post&utm\_medium=web&showWelcomeOnShare=false](https://open.substack.com/pub/abhinavyerramreddy/p/caught-stealing-surprise-after-surprise?r=38m95e&utm_campaign=post&utm_medium=web&showWelcomeOnShare=false)

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I don’t think Darren Aronofksy committed enough to the bit, for this to be a truly memorable experience. It tries to create a Big Lebowski/After Hours type movie about a regular dude getting swept up in an increasingly bizarre series of circumstances, but it is nowhere near as witty and wild as either of those films.

As it is, it’s a fine time to spend at the movies, but nothing truly special.
  - Though I agree that there's definitely elements of The Big Lebowski and After Hours in the movie, to me it felt more like he was going for a turn-of-the-century Guy Ritchie/Danny Boyle style film. Shallow Grave, Trainspotting, Lock Stock, Snatch. In either case, he didn't nail it and the end-product feels unlike those movies as well as very un-Aronofsky
  - This is the answer. A big ol’ cinematic “meh”. And when you’ve come from the heights of where Arronofsky’s been and that stacked cast it just feels like a waste.
2. Personally, I didn’t like the movie very much. But—aside from Pi—I prefer my Aronofsky big and boisterous. I’m the guy that likes Noah and The Fountain and Mother!

As far as the broader public, this kinda low budget, hardboiled noir is very successful on streaming. I don’t think it does as well in theaters anymore. The Nice Guys, which I love, was also a bomb, so I don’t think this response is new.
3. Probably bad marketing and a confusing trailer. The trailer made it look like there were a lot of comedic beats, and quirky characters. I have A-List so I took a chance on seeing it. I was not expecting how dark, and violent it was. The trailer made that initial mistaken confrontation in the hallway seem like it was played for laughs, yet in the movie he was beat so viciously he lost a kidney. The different factions looked like they were silly caricatures and were instead vicious killers. I enjoyed the movie, but man it was not what I expected at all.

4. Going to be a very controversial take but, I think that basically a lot of people who are big cinema fans have sort of lost a lot of love for Aronofsky.

Not including *Postcards From Earth*, the last three Aronofsky films (*Noah*, *mother!* and *The Whale*) all were fairly mixed received by folks. *mother!* in particular was pretty widely mocked for it's unpleasantness and incredibly on-the-nose storytelling. Even those who liked *The Whale* mostly only cared about it because of Brendan Fraser rather than the story and script, which again, was considered to be fairly cliche and unsubtle.
5. I didn’t see it, but to me it looked like DA doing his best impression of a Guy Richie film. In the end it looked like trodden territory. Perhaps it was genius and had a bad trailer, but I was really disappointed by Mother so put me in the ‘blip’ column.
6. He’s entered his David O. Russell period. Movies with great casts, good scripts, and competent but bland execution, resulting in “fine” movies that become forgotten before you hit the parking lot.
7. There’s too much tonal dissonance between the quirkiness of its comedic side and the darkness of its crime-thriller side to sit well with mass audiences who wanted something more Lebowski-esque; and while it was a solid success with the more critical audience, it also had the misfortune to come out shortly before *One Battle After Another*, which just completely overshadowed it with that crowd. So that momentum carried away people who would still be talking about it, and they moved on and never looked back.

## 3. What makes watching anything with Philip Seymour Hoffman so engrossing?

**Score:** 355
**Body:** Hi Folks, i love watching artistic driven films with depths and more arthouse style such as french and European films in general.

I'm no Film snob but I'm very fascinated by Phillip Seymour Hoffman. He seems to me to be character actor. His acting style and choice of projects seem to be more European style. I absolutely loved him in the Master. But also in all his films.

I lack any film analysis training or experience so I'm wondering what makes Phillip Seymour Hoffman such engrossing and spellbinding actor? He genuinely comes across very different than most American actors.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Take a look at what Hoffman does here: ["Are You Threatening Me?"](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=phfjlcGlT3g)

Sandler's great in that scene too, but the way the two interact summarises the delirious way in which human exchanges commonly escalate.

If we're thinking PTA, one could compare this scene with the "Comrade Josh" phone dialogue from ONE BATTLE. Leo DiCaprio does "furious middle-aged man" brilliantly, but what DiCaprio doesn't do is convey the possibility of absolute, absurd violence the way Hoffman does. Humans are simple creatures deep down: we struggle to look away from the threat.

So for me what was distinctive about Hoffman was his ability to _become the threat_ and to impart all the intensities of that fundamental volatility of human relations—including self-relation.  
  
In many of Hoffman's best scenes it's like you're watching someone either undergo or produce a traumatic break from normalcy. And I gather that may have had something to do with what it was like to be him, as he was notoriously if intermittently troubled off-screen.

Compare, too, Nicolas Cage. Cage is also famous for chewing the scenery, but his moments of rage attract laughter and pity.

Yes, you could simply say "Hoffman had wonderful range", and he did. He could and did play extraordinarily diverse roles. But his acting talent went beyond versatile shapeshifting. He was distinguished by *where* he was able to go emotionally, and how he could accelerate between points of character.
  - > Take a look at what Hoffman does here: "Are You Threatening Me?"

That little air punch he does with the phone in his hand after Sandler begins to argue with him  is such a great moment.
    - I still pull out his "SHUT, SHUT, SHUT, SHUT, SHUT UP!" from time to time
2. I have no intellectual understanding of why Hoffman was so great, but I think his X factor must have been his total emotional commitment to every role. There was no cliff he wouldn't dive off, and he was never afraid to look pathetic or ridiculous. John le Carré wrote an obituary in which he remarked that he wasn't surprised Hoffman met a tragic end because of how intensely he threw himself into everything he did. (Le Carré also said he thought Hoffman was the only American who could play George Smiley... jesus christ I wish we could've seen that). But he did it while also maintaining the craft and good taste to pull it all off. 

Another part of his success was just how he fit into the Hollywood casting process. He had the right voice and physique to play schlubby supporting roles, but he had the versatility to play *every kind* of schlubby supporting role. Even just the range of his first three PT Anderson roles is spellbinding, but then you look at what he did in "mercenary" roles like Mission Impossible 3 and The Hunger Games. He was able to keep fantastical characters grounded and make mundane characters vivid. 

At this point I'm just gushing... and also making myself sad at what could've been. But you might enjoy this [interview](https://youtu.be/m9NjCuG7uS8?si=k15447BDFYq8rAio) Hoffman did on *Capote*, where he discusses his interpretation of Truman Capote's personal tragedy and why it was necessary for the role to have some emotional context from his own life.
  - I love this sub, thank you for sharing that wonderful interview, hadn’t seen that.
  - You have captured his essence very well here with your reply. He was an everyday man whilst also he could transform into larger than life characters so effortlessly. Great point about Le Carrre attesting to Hoffman's ability and talent. very good analysis and feedback i will remember this.
3. I mean, this is the boring overly simple answer, but he makes interesting decisions and commits to them 100%. That brings an energy, clarity and authenticity to his performance. His interesting acting choices are matched by his interesting choices in which roles to take. So you get this guy who really isn’t a chameleon but is playing this incredibly wide spectrum of types of characters and everything from Brent’s nervous giggle to Bunny in Lebowski to him rambling about why he likes smoking Kool Cigs in The Master reads as genuine and true. That authenticity sticks with people.
4. You should watch Love Liza (2002) if you like him, brings me to tears every time. Such an “under the radar” movie. The way he plays the part is so heart wrenching, every time I watch it I get the same reaction. I think I like the parts where you can kinda see the internal struggle in him, that’s something he did well and probably what lead to his captivating performances.
5. I think he was drawing upon a lot of psychological sadness so his emotion always felt really genuine; it really drew you towards him on the screen.

When he goes off on one against Tom Hanks in Charlie Wilson's War, I genuinely felt like he had been learning Finnish; I felt it. To caricature him, he often had a great ability to explode but I think his real talent was being able to implode, and for that to be fully viewable on his body and face.   
  
When he silently tips over the coffee table in quiet rage (in Owning Mahowny or Before the Devil Knows your Dead; I cannot remember); he really doesn't need to do more. His subtext is so present. At the end of A Most Wanted Man too, he almost falls down and has to catch himself; it delivers so much in so little.
6. I'll give a quick thing I think about everytime I watch him. 

I always can feel the weight of the past on his character for some reason I dont know why that is?

Its a rare quality its hard to quantify. I never look at Brad Pitt Leo Pacino Nicholson and feel that same thing. I always see a great actor acting but with Hoffman Day Lewis Gary Oldman etc im watching a new guy being or behaving.

Maybe its the difference between "character actor" and leading man. idk. 

But feeling the weight of the past is something I always think about when thinking about Hoffman. The processing scene in The Master might be the greatest acting scene I have ever seen

## 4. It is remarkable how good Spielberg's films used to look

**Score:** 343
**Body:** I've been watching the Indiana Jones trilogy and I guess I forgot just how good these movies look, even the maligned Temple of Doom.

Then I think about some of his other blockbusters such as Jaws, ET, Jurassic Park, as well as Close Encounters. These are some of the best looking films ever, let alone blockbusters.

Spielberg's older movies were so visually pulsating with color and energy. It was like after Jurassic Park in 1993, the life was drained dry. At least I can appreciate that we still have these older films on a variety of formats available to watch.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I thought his last two films, *The Fablemans* and the *West Side Story* remake had some strong cinematography.

*Ready Player One* did look like a live-action video game rather than a movie, so it's up to you if you want to interpret that as a bad thing or not.
  - Spielberg's pull and vision has allowed him to develop very fruitful and consistent relationships with brilliant cinematographers.

Everything Spielberg has done since SCHINDLER'S LIST has been photographed by Janusz Kamiński, who's a bit of a legend by now. It's notable how desaturated some of the colour grading was on some of these features 20 years ago (eg MINORITY REPORT) whereas the recent works have had a warmer, more teal and orange look, albeit not too excessive.

The Indiana Jones trilogy was all shot by the same DP, Douglas Slocombe, whereas most of Spielberg's other 80s features were shot by Allen Daviau.
    - Dougie Slocombe was from the generation that not only learned what would look good without ever having access to a monitor, he learned what it would look like in Black & White. Purely with his eyes and his imagination. So he creates separation within the frame in a way that no one from later eras seems capable of (even the great Janusz Kaminski who shot Crystal Skull trying to emulate him). 

Those first three films look so damn good!
  - That one shot in *Fablemans* with young Sammy projecting the camera onto his hand was **everywhere** in marketing and commercials for the Oscars
2. I was watching Gremlins last month. And while he’s only an EP and it’s a Dante effort (who is a visual wizard (albeit of the mad variety) in his own right)… i was shocked at how fucking amazing the film looked. 

The wooden den of Billy’s attic based bedroom, the low lighting with any mogwai, even the mogwai themselves… it all looks so wonderfully lived in, real, and it just pops in frame. 

I had more fun just “looking” at the film than I did watching the story and performances.

The ugliest piece of (big budget) filmed entertainment I have seen in the last 5 years was the Obi-Wan miniseries. It was crystal clear and looked so faux and with limited vision and mise-en-scene. 

It was shocking to be reminded of what a run of the mill film could look like.
  - I think a lot of this is probably that we're completely tired with modern flat lighting and everything-is-CGI look. A couple weeks ago I watched the original Suspiria and it felt like my eyes were having an orgasm.
3. I love that whole era of Universal Studios aesthetic. I grew up on all those movies & then going on the studio tour tram at Universal Studios Hollywood learning all about how these movies were made & who made them. Spielberg embodies that whole magic of that era imo. Somebody else who has that classic look is John Landis.

5. Kaminski hate in this thread is crazy. Schindler's List, Saving Private Ryan, Catch Me If You Can, Munich, Lincoln or West Side Story are some of Spielberg's best looking movies, to name a few. And while I don't necessarily like everything they made in the last 30 years, I can't hate Kaminski because he's one of the few DP's that try to do something interesting in a world where every movie looks almost the same. 6. Not really a "discussion" comment but since you brought up Indiana Jones, from which almost every shot is burned into my brain because I love those movies so much, here's one of my favorites:

[Mola Ram during the second sacrifice](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hbvosK86F1A#t=2m31s)

This shot is everything. It's got the ominous chants, the terrifying red lighting, Amrish Puri's sinister look of victory. In Temple's cinematic structure it's basically the complete nadir for Indiana Jones. He's enslaved by the blood of Kali and Willie is about to be killed, and Mola Ram is relishing the victory. It's no surprise the very next shot is Short Round breaking out of his chains, which is the turn into the third act and, IMO, the best non-stop 30 minutes of action in any of the original three movies (as much as I love all of them).

## 5. The Running Man: why would a unique director wilfully become generic?

**Score:** 310
**Body:** I watched The Running Man earlier in the week, and thought it was a perfectly functional, decent action-comedy. I also can’t remember the last time I’ve been more baffled by the existence of a film, to the point where it felt like a director was performing self-sabotage for us all to see. When I watch a bad film from the likes of Zack Snyder, I can still understand the intent behind the piece, and the fact that as a director, he is playing to his own sensibilities.

But The Running Man is the kind of disappointment that truly baffles me. Edgar Wright is a highly distinctive director, and unlike Wes Anderson, there has been no expression of fatigue from the audience, whatsoever. Despite this, Wright decided to forsake his own style. I appreciated Last Night in SoHo (even though it jumped the shark in the final act), because there, it still felt like Wright traded off his style to try something distinctive. But with The Running Man, it truly baffles me to no end why a director like Wright would, as a conscious decision, make a film that feels like a David Leitch B-side.

There are two moments in this film which show that, to no surprise, Wright is still capable of bringing his unique sensibilities to the film. One which involves bullets fired to the beat of a song, and another in which Glen Powell’s face moves in the same direction as his face on a TV behind him. Both moments were shown in the trailer, and are not indicative at all of the rest of the film. If Wright is still capable of creating such moments (I mean, why wouldn’t he be?) then why choose not to? Again, I would understand and support if he abandoned his traditional style to aspire to something (like what PTA did with One Battle After Another, or Spielberg with Schindler’s List), but this is not one of those instances. It is a talented director striving toward mediocrity.

EDIT: My [full review](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=WZulA36AdGU) of the movie!

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I think a big problem is that he made an unnecessary rod for his own back by sticking to the formula of the book too much.

The book is a depressing, dystopian tale of a desperate and sickly man who survives more often than not by being shuttled around, and hidden by others.

Wright wanted to make a zany action comedy about a tough, charismatic guy with anger issues, who fights his way out of trouble.

The problem is that he tried to map the latter concept onto the former, despite the fact it can't support it, and made little to no changes to accomodate it. It'd be like shooting The Shining as a comedy. But changing nothing fundamental about the story or structure. None of your beats are going to work.

Wright was simply a bad fit for the material. Even the 80s version (which has plenty of one-liners and satire) understands that the material doesn't work unless everyone involved treats the stakes as deathly serious.

But Wright (as much as I love him) seemingly can't make a film unless it's drenched in irony. Everyone is a cartoon character. And this works when the script and story are in perfect harmony with his vision. But when it's fighting tooth and nail against it, you get this. Neither fish nor fowl.

It's the equivalent of steak-flavoured ice cream. Two things that are great in isolation. But absolutely do not work together. Meaning the project was fundamentally misconceived from the start. It just couldn't pick a lane.
  - These were my thoughts too on watching it. It's a fascinating example of the challenges in adapting a novel to screen. People have long criticised the 1987 version for "being nothing like the book" but I've always felt it was brilliant in how it distilled the core ideas into something that worked much more cinematically for that time. Wright was so restrained in trying to please/impress King while also trying hard to please his audience. The two sensibilities cancel each other out for the most part, leading to a strangely moot, grey experience.  


But my biggest confusion is the ending. How could it follow the book so closely, but then botch the ending so absolutely terribly? It has no resonance or coherence, it's just flat-out awful. I'm aware he was still editing it up to the later half of October, he must have had some truly tortuous nights if that ending is the best they could put together. 
    - That was my partners take. That ending just killed everything that came before it. It never hit high points but it was a great representation of the core story. Until the end when it decided to become something it wasn’t.
2. He’s been wanting to adapt The Running Man for a while. So clearly his heart was in it at some point.

Unfortunately the movie just didn’t come together. You ask why a director would make the conscious decision to make a mediocre movie (assuming that’s what you meant by “David Leitch b-side” — unless you really liked Bullet Train) … I mean, they usually don’t? 

I think what may have happened is that he made the decision to tone down his usual style from the get-go, but because this was a big studio blockbuster, he was also pressured to make some changes. I have a strong suspicion the (horrible!) ending was the result of some last minute rewrites and reshoots but who knows. Maybe that was always the intent, and he was just misguided.
3. 'unlike Wes Anderson, there has been no expression of fatigue from the audience, whatsoever'

Anderson has actually become a much more consistent performer, even if on a mid-tier level, than Wright. Wright is less productive than Anderson, and seems to have even lost his cult following.
4. Filmmaking is really fucking hard.


And the attention to detail in his films is really a lot of work.


At some point, you just don't have the energy or the inclination to keep doing the same thing over and over just because that's what people expect.


That's without going into the business side of things, which can limit time and freedom.
  - When you’re dealing with a decent budget you have to answer to the money supply
5. Sometimes you do a film for the check or to establish a relationship for a film you want to do later. Sometimes the producers have final say and you just do it and move on. He will be 100% okay.
6. I haven’t seen it yet, but I read the book recently to prepare. From previews it looks like it’s trying to be fun. The book is bleak as hell. 

To me, this is like turning Cormac McCarthy’s The Road into a buddy comedy.

Plus Glen Powell sucks.
7. The script has its fair share of setups that get paid off and the action scenes are well staged with some clever camera moves, it doesn't feel like Wright's strengths were completely erased to me

When it comes to the characters, the pace, and especially the last 5 minutes of the movie though, it really struggles 

There's something about passion projects like this and like Del Toro's Frankenstein where the emotional core and energy of the film get lost along the way
